text: Robotic splenic cyst fenestration. The patient is a fifty year old female with chronic left upper quadrant pain and postprandial fullness. She had a history of asthma and morbid obesity. A CT abdomen pelvis was obtained for her preoperative workup showing a thirteen centimeter splenic cyst with benign features. This is a selection of the axial cuts from the CT scan showing an eleven by thirteen centimeter splenic cyst with benign features. No other abnormalities of the abdomen or pelvis were identified. The coronal cuts again show the large splenic cyst with displacement of the normal splenic parenchyma.

The surgeon places four 8mm robotic trocars across the upper abdomen for port placement. Laparoscopic scissors are used to open the splenic cyst, aspirating approximately 850 cc of brown fluid. The robot is docked, and dense adhesions to the abdominal wall, liver, and omentum are lysed. Endocyanine green dye is administered to visualize the interface between the splenic parenchyma and cyst capsule. A monopolar hook marks the margin, followed by the vessel sealer dividing the anterior cyst wall from the splenic parenchyma.

The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) dye to identify the border between the cyst capsule and the spleen at the superior medial aspect. The final attachments are divided with a vessel sealer. The cyst cavity is irrigated and checked for hemostasis, with a bleeding vessel identified and treated using bipolar cautery. The cavity is irrigated again to ensure hemostasis.

An omental patch is placed to fill the cavity, and a 19 French Blake drain is inserted. An 8mm trocar is removed, and the site is enlarged to allow for the placement of a laparoscopic retrieval bag, which is used to remove the specimen.

Final pathology returned as dense fibrous tissue consistent with benign splenic pseudocyst. At the time of follow-up, her left upper quadrant pain and postprandial fullness had significantly improved.
